answer:
**Charles Comte discusses property rights in inventions and industrial procedures primarily in Chapter 29 ("On the property of inventions or industrial processes") of *Traité de la propriété Vol. II*.**[^1]

Rather than arguing strongly *for* exclusive property rights as a natural or perpetual entitlement (which he critiques extensively as a flawed analogy to the occupation of material goods, leading to absurdities like perpetual monopolies on basic innovations such as bread-making or footwear), Comte concedes a limited, non-exclusive natural right: **"Every person who makes a discovery in the arts certainly has the right to exploit it for his profit."**[^2] He stresses this requires no special government act beyond general guarantees of liberty and property; the *exclusive* privilege (i.e., patents) is instead a temporary *monopoly* granted by statute, justified only hypothetically for encouragement and inferior to free industry.[^3] Chapter 30 details the relevant laws (English, French, American), treating them as restrictions on liberty rather than inherent property.[^4]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété Vol. II*, [Chapter 29](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-ii-6beea5/29-de-la-propriete-des-inventions-ou-des-procedes-ind/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété Vol. II*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-ii-6beea5/29-de-la-propriete-des-inventions-ou-des-procedes-ind/translation/#p-0668a8).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété Vol. II*, [paragraphs](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-ii-6beea5/29-de-la-propriete-des-inventions-ou-des-procedes-ind/translation/#p-0668a8) from p-0668a8 through p-d81732 (right to exploit vs. exclusion).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété Vol. II*, [Chapter 30](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-ii-6beea5/30-des-lois-relatives-a-la-propriete-des-inventions-i/translation/).
